title: AP World History: Modern DBQ Practice Exam 02

questions.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which abolition and emancipation transformed labor systems in the Atlantic and Indian Ocean worlds during the nineteenth century.
Document 1
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Testimony of Joseph Martin, formerly enslaved dockworker, to a colonial labor commission, 1840, Bridgetown, Barbados.
Source Type: Testimony.
“Freedom has changed the name by which the planter calls us, but the estate still controls much of our time. Some men rent small plots and work for wages when they choose, but others must accept the planter’s terms because the best land remains in the hands of the old masters. We no longer fear the whip as before, yet hunger can compel a man nearly as strongly.”
Sourcing cues: Formerly enslaved worker speaking after emancipation; emphasizes lived experience and the limits of formal freedom.
Document 2
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Letter from a Cuban sugar planter to a business partner, 1866, Matanzas, Cuba.
Source Type: Private business letter.
“The talk of abolition grows louder each year, and the price of field hands has become uncertain. Our mills cannot stand idle. If Africans cannot be obtained as before, we must look to contract laborers, machines, and stricter accounting of every hour worked. Sugar rewards discipline, not sentiment.”
Sourcing cues: Plantation owner concerned with profits; reveals elite attempts to preserve plantation production despite pressure against slavery.
Document 3
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Recruitment notice posted by a labor contractor near Calcutta, 1874, British India.
Source Type: Recruitment notice.
“Men and women willing to work overseas may sign contracts for five years on plantations in Mauritius, Natal, or the Caribbean. Passage will be provided. Wages, rations, and return passage after the term shall be recorded before the magistrate. Workers must obey plantation rules during the period of contract.”
Sourcing cues: Official-style recruitment language; presents indenture as contractual while minimizing power imbalance and coercive conditions.
Document 4
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Colonial migration report, 1838–1880.
Source Type: Plain-text data table.
Selected Contract Labor Migration after Emancipation, 1838–1880
Destination Colony Main Origin of Workers Approximate Pattern Described
Mauritius India Large plantation labor migration after slavery ended
British Guiana India Sugar estates expanded indentured workforce
Trinidad India Contract workers used on sugar plantations
Natal India Workers recruited for plantations and public works
Cuba China and Yucatán Contract labor used alongside slavery and wage labor
Peru China Workers recruited for plantations, railways, and guano extraction
Note on the table: Contracts often promised wages and return passage, but workers frequently faced debt, restrictions on movement, and harsh discipline.
Sourcing cues: Colonial administrative data; useful for identifying large-scale labor movements but less detailed about workers’ personal experiences.
Document 5
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Decree issued by the Sultan of Zanzibar under British diplomatic pressure, 1897, Zanzibar.
Source Type: Government decree.
“Any person held as a slave within the dominions of Zanzibar may present a claim before the proper authority and receive a certificate of freedom. No person shall be newly bought or sold. Former masters may retain lawful property, but they may not compel freed persons to remain without agreement.”
Sourcing cues: Government decree shaped by outside pressure; shows formal legal change but may not reveal enforcement on plantations or in households.
Document 6
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Described photograph from a sugar estate, Mauritius, c. 1890.
Source Type: Described photograph.
The photograph shows rows of cane workers standing beside a narrow-gauge rail line used to move cane to a mill. Most workers wear simple cotton clothing and hold cutting tools. A European estate manager stands apart on horseback near a ledger clerk. In the background are barracks-like worker housing and a smoking mill chimney.
Sourcing cues: Visual evidence of plantation organization after emancipation; shows hierarchy, industrial machinery, and disciplined field labor but not workers’ voices.
Document 7
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Report by an East African merchant to a coastal commercial council, 1888, Mombasa.
Source Type: Commercial report.
“Caravan labor is harder to secure since foreign patrols and new regulations interfere with the old traffic in captives. Ivory, cloves, and grain still require carriers, porters, and household servants. Some freed persons now hire themselves by the month, but many remain tied to patrons through debt, marriage arrangements, or protection.”
Sourcing cues: Merchant perspective from a changing Indian Ocean economy; focuses on labor supply and commercial continuity rather than moral reform.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 7
Thesis / Claim — 1 point: Earns the point by making a historically defensible claim that evaluates the extent to which abolition and emancipation transformed nineteenth-century labor systems. A strong thesis should establish a line of reasoning, such as arguing that legal slavery declined but coercive plantation, contract, debt, and dependent labor systems continued in new forms.
Contextualization — 1 point: Earns the point by describing a broader historical context relevant to abolition and labor change, such as Enlightenment ideas, Atlantic revolutions, slave resistance, British abolitionism, industrial capitalism, plantation commodity production, imperial expansion, or the growth of global demand for sugar, cotton, guano, cloves, and other commodities.
Evidence from the Documents — 2 points: Earns 1 point by accurately using the content of at least three documents to address the prompt. Earns 2 points by using at least six documents to support an argument about legal emancipation, plantation continuity, indentured migration, contract labor, debt, wage labor, imperial pressure, or changing labor control.
Evidence Beyond the Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by using at least one specific piece of relevant outside evidence not found in the documents, such as the Haitian Revolution, the British abolition of the slave trade in 1807, British emancipation in 1833, the apprenticeship system, Brazilian abolition in 1888, Russian serf emancipation, Indian indentured labor in Fiji, anti-slavery naval patrols, or abolitionist religious movements.
Sourcing / Analysis of Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by explaining how or why the point of view, purpose, audience, or historical situation of at least three documents is relevant to the argument. Effective examples include explaining why former enslaved workers emphasized incomplete freedom, why planters looked for replacement labor, why recruitment notices made indenture appear orderly, why decrees may show formal law more than enforcement, or why merchants focused on labor supply rather than human rights.
Complexity — 1 point: Earns the point by demonstrating a more complex understanding of labor transformation. This may include explaining regional variation between the Atlantic and Indian Ocean worlds, distinguishing legal status from actual working conditions, showing how abolition was shaped by both moral reform and economic interests, or analyzing how industrial and plantation capitalism adapted to the end of legal slavery.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong DBQ could argue that abolition transformed labor by ending or weakening legal slave ownership in many regions, but that plantation owners, merchants, and imperial states developed new systems of labor control, including indenture, wage dependency, debt, and contract restrictions. Documents 1, 5, and 7 are useful for showing partial change and limits of freedom. Documents 2, 3, 4, and 6 help show continuity in plantation production and the rise of migrant contract labor. Outside evidence could include the Haitian Revolution, British emancipation, apprenticeship, Brazilian abolition, or Indian indentured migration to Fiji and the Caribbean. Strong sourcing might analyze the interests of planters, the promotional purpose of recruitment notices, the limits of government decrees, and the perspective of formerly enslaved workers.
